result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up after something ends.

Not the dramatic kind—the slammed doors, the final words echoing like they matter more than they do—but the softer quiet that settles in later, when everything that used to make noise simply… doesn’t anymore. It’s the absence of small things. Notifications that stop. A chair that stays empty. The way your thoughts no longer orbit someone else, and for a while, don’t seem to know where to go instead.

People talk about closure like it’s a door you can shut cleanly, like a decisive click and then you’re done. But most endings aren’t built like that. They’re more like rooms you slowly stop entering. At first you stand in the doorway a lot, remembering where everything used to be. Then you pass by in the hallway and glance in. Eventually, one day, you realize you walked past without looking at all.

That realization is strange. Not triumphant, not tragic—just unfamiliar. Like finding out you’ve been carrying something heavy for so long that you forgot what it felt like to move without it.

And then comes the unexpected part: you start filling the space.

Not all at once. Not intentionally, even. A new song that sticks for no particular reason. A habit of walking a different route home. A conversation that lingers a little longer than usual. These small, almost forgettable things begin to stitch together into something that feels, quietly, like a life continuing.

There’s no ceremony for this phase. No clear marker that says, “You’re past it now.” It just happens in increments so subtle you almost miss them.

Until one day, the quiet doesn’t feel like absence anymore.

It feels like room.
:::writing
